dp.label: dp[i] = ways to reach step i

frames.caption: Seed the base cases: dp[0] = 1 (one empty way) and dp[1] = 1 (a single step). The table fills left to right from here.
frames.note: Every DP starts from base cases that need no recurrence. To reach step 0 there is exactly one way — do nothing — so ways(0)=1; to reach step 1 there is one way — a single step — so ways(1)=1. These two seeds are all the recurrence below ever needs, because each later cell looks back exactly two places. (Note the shift: ways(2)=2 equals fib(3) in the 0-indexed sequence 0,1,1,2,3,5,8 — the off-by-one is the only thing that trips people up.)

frames.caption: dp[2] = dp[1] + dp[0] = 1 + 1 = 2.
frames.note: The transition is the whole idea: to land on step i your last move was either a single step from i-1 or a double step from i-2, and those two routes are disjoint and cover every possibility — so ways(i) = ways(i-1) + ways(i-2). We ADD the two cells immediately to the left because the count of distinct paths splits cleanly by "what was the final move". This is literally Fibonacci; tabulating it bottom-up turns the naive O(2^n) recursion (which re-expands the same calls over and over) into a single O(n) sweep.

frames.caption: dp[3] = dp[2] + dp[1] = 2 + 1 = 3.
frames.note: The transition is the whole idea: to land on step i your last move was either a single step from i-1 or a double step from i-2, and those two routes are disjoint and cover every possibility — so ways(i) = ways(i-1) + ways(i-2). We ADD the two cells immediately to the left because the count of distinct paths splits cleanly by "what was the final move". This is literally Fibonacci; tabulating it bottom-up turns the naive O(2^n) recursion (which re-expands the same calls over and over) into a single O(n) sweep.

frames.caption: dp[4] = dp[3] + dp[2] = 3 + 2 = 5.
frames.note: The transition is the whole idea: to land on step i your last move was either a single step from i-1 or a double step from i-2, and those two routes are disjoint and cover every possibility — so ways(i) = ways(i-1) + ways(i-2). We ADD the two cells immediately to the left because the count of distinct paths splits cleanly by "what was the final move". This is literally Fibonacci; tabulating it bottom-up turns the naive O(2^n) recursion (which re-expands the same calls over and over) into a single O(n) sweep.

frames.caption: dp[5] = dp[4] + dp[3] = 5 + 3 = 8.
frames.note: Look at what each cell actually reads: only the previous two. Nothing earlier is ever consulted again, so the whole array is wasteful — you can keep just two rolling variables (the reviewer's prev2 and prev1) and slide them forward, collapsing O(n) space to O(1). The table here makes the data dependency visible; the shipped C# is exactly this table with everything but the last two cells thrown away.

frames.caption: Answer is dp[5] = 8 — there are 8 distinct ways to climb 5 stairs.
frames.note: The last cell holds the answer: dp[n] is the number of distinct ways to climb all n stairs. We filled each cell once from O(1) earlier cells, so the cost is O(n) time — an exponential speedup over the naive recursion tree. This dp[i]=dp[i-1]+dp[i-2] skeleton (Fibonacci shifted by one) is the template for the whole 1D-DP family: House Robber, Decode Ways, and friends just change what the transition combines.
